statement.p: Determine whether each set is a vector space or not.

match.premise: All vectors in \mathbb{R}^3 with the usual vector addition and scalar multiplication.
The set of all polynomials \mathbb{P}_2 with real coefficients of degree at most 2.
The set of all real-valued functions f such that f(0)=0.
The set of all vectors in \mathbb{R}^2 with positive entries under the usual addition and scalar multiplication.
The set of all polynomials with constant term equal to 1 under the usual addition and scalar multiplication.
The set of all real 2 \times 2 matrices with determinant 1 under the usual matrix addition and scalar multiplication.

statement.p: Consider the set \mathbb{R}^2 with the following non-standard addition operation \oplus: (a,b)\oplus(c,d)=(a+d,b+c). Scalar multiplication is defined in the usual way. Is this a vector space? Explain why or why not.

Consider \mathbb{R}^2 with the following non-standard addition operation \oplus: (a,b)\oplus(c,d)=(0,b+d). Scalar multiplication is defined in the usual way. Is this a vector space? Explain why or why not.
Consider \mathbb{R}^2 with the following non-standard scalar multiplication: c\odot(a,b)=(a,cb). Vector addition is defined as usual. Is this a vector space? Explain why or why not.
Consider \mathbb{R}^2 with the following non-standard addition operation \oplus: (a,b)\oplus(c,d)=(a-c,b-d). Scalar multiplication is defined as usual. Is this a vector space? Explain why or why not.

Prove that the set \mathrm{Seq}_{\mathbb{R}} from is a vector space. Hint: this is a special case of , if you realize that a sequence (a_i)_{i\in\mathbb{N}} is the same thing as a function a:\mathbb{N}\to \mathbb{R}.
Prove that the set \mathbb{P} from is a vector space.
Let V be the set of functions defined on a set X that have values in a vector space W. Is this a vector space? Explain.

Consider the set \mathbb{R}^2 with the following non-standard operations of addition and scalar multiplication:
(a,b)\oplus(c,d) = (a+c-1, b+d-1),
k\odot(c,d) = (kc+(1-k), kd+(1-k)).
Show that \mathbb{R}^2 is a vector space with these operations. Hint: the zero vector is not (0,0), but (1,1).

Consider the set \mathbb{R} of real numbers. Addition of real numbers is defined in the usual way, and scalar multiplication is just multiplication of one real number by another. In other words, x+y means to add the two numbers and xy means to multiply them. Show that \mathbb{R}, with these operations, is a real vector space.

Let K=\mathbb{Q} be the field of rational numbers, and let V be the set of real numbers of the form a+b\sqrt{2}, where a and b are rational numbers. Show that with the usual operations, V is a \mathbb{Q}-vector space.
